claims.text: Any challenge to established scientific dogma is labeled pseudoscience, which is arrogant because today's pseudoscience sometimes becomes tomorrow's science.

evidence.quote:
any challenge of established scientific
dogma
is labeled pseudoscience isn't that a
bit arrogant
i mean isn't pseudoscience only
pseudoscience until i

claims.rationale: Rhetorical framing conflates legitimate scientific provisionalism with the 'pseudoscience becomes science' trope. In practice, mainstream science does reclassify findings when evidence supports them (plate tectonics, heliobacter, etc.), but the reclassification happens through the very methods the host frames as dogma. The claim's directTruth is low as stated — it's closer to a strawman of how scientific skepticism works.

claims.text: Kepler and Copernicus were ridiculed by their peers for their ideas.

evidence.quote:
science kepler and copernicus
were ridiculed by their peers
louis pasteur's research was rejected by
the medical establishment
gregor mendel nikola tesla initi

claims.rationale: Kepler and Copernicus faced opposition primarily from Church authorities and Aristotelian philosophers, not from a unified peer-review system (which did not exist). Copernicus's De Revolutionibus was in fact initially received by many astronomers as a useful computational tool. The 'ridiculed by peers' framing oversimplifies the reception.

claims.text: Louis Pasteur's research was rejected by the medical establishment before being accepted.

evidence.quote:
peers
louis pasteur's research was rejected by
the medical establishment
gregor mendel nikola tesla initially
they w

claims.rationale: Pasteur's germ theory of disease did face significant resistance from physicians in the 1860s-70s committed to miasma and spontaneous generation. He ultimately won over the establishment through decisive experiments (anthrax, rabies). The host's framing is broadly correct but obscures that he was accepted within his own lifetime.

claims.text: In 1903 the New York Times said manned flight would not be possible for 1 to 10 million years.

evidence.quote:
matured since then
think about this in 1903 when the wright
brothers and others were building
airplanes
the new york times said manned flight
would not be possible for 1

claims.rationale: A 1903 New York Times editorial (published October 9, 1903) did dismiss manned flight as requiring 'from one to ten million years' — this is a real and verifiable quote, cited frequently as an example of press techno-pessimism. Published just nine weeks before the Wright Brothers' successful flight at Kitty Hawk on December 17, 1903.

claims.text: In 1985 the New York Times said no matter how inexpensive or powerful a laptop computer would become, nobody would want one.

the new york times said no matter how
inexpensive or powerful a laptop
computer would become
nobody would want one no boy and when
a

claims.rationale: Erik Sandberg-Diment's December 8, 1985 NYT column 'The Executive Computer' did make this claim, pronouncing laptops unnecessary. Widely cited and verifiable. The quote is slightly paraphrased but substantively accurate.

claims.text: When Apple announced the iPhone, the New York Times said Apple would probably never build it and if they did, nobody would buy it.

evidence.quote:
and when
apple announced the iphone
the time said they'd probably never
build it and even if they did
nobody would buy it and speaking of
twitter
here's what th

claims.rationale: This claim does not match any well-documented NYT piece. There were skeptical pieces from various columnists at the time, but the specific quote 'probably never build it... nobody would buy it' attributed to 'The Times' in this formulation is not verifiable. Host rhetorically lumps multiple critiques together.

claims.text: When Twitter launched, the New York Times compared using it for literate communication to 'firing up a CB radio and hearing some guy recite the Iliad'.

evidence.quote:
itter
here's what the new york times said when
it launched using
twitter for literate communication is
about as likely as
firing up a cb radio and hearing some
guy recite the iliad

claims.rationale: The 'CB radio / Iliad' line is real — from a NYT piece by Alex Beam (published Times and other outlets 2007 era). The specific phrasing is verifiable. The framing that NYT 'said when it launched' is loose — early Twitter coverage was mixed rather than uniformly dismissive.

claims.text: Suggesting that levitating heavy objects with sound or creating free energy is possible results in vicious attacks from both social media and the New York Times.

evidence.quote:
't trust
the media you have
if you suggest that levitating heavy
objects with sound
or creating free energy is even possible
you're viciously attacked
not just

claims.rationale: Host conflates two claims: (1) acoustic levitation of small objects is a real, demonstrated phenomenon with peer-reviewed physics (not attacked). (2) 'Free energy' / perpetual motion violates thermodynamics and is legitimately criticized as pseudoscience. Bundling them as equivalently-persecuted ideas misrepresents both. The 'vicious attack' framing is self-serving.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the 'pseudoscience-today, science-tomorrow' thesis with specific examples.

claims.text: Corporate media and social media make money by getting users to attack each other, which is their business model.

evidence.quote:
ia or social media
they make money by getting all of us to
attack each other that's their business
model
it's shameful and that's why nobody
trusts them who cares

claims.rationale: The engagement-drives-outrage hypothesis has empirical support (Haidt, Rozado, internal Facebook docs). 'Business model' is overstated — platforms optimize for engagement of which outrage is one vector. Corporate media economics are more varied than 'get users to attack each other'. Partially true, overbroad.

claims.rationale: The quote is genuine — from Shaw's 1919 play 'Annajanska, the Bolshevik Empress'. Accurately attributed. Verifiable.

claims.rationale: Pure value judgment / rallying rhetoric. No empirical content to calibrate directTruth against — comparing societal contributions of inventors vs a newspaper is not a well-formed factual question. Omitting directTruth.

claims.text: Gregor Mendel and Nikola Tesla were initially rejected before their work was vindicated.

evidence.quote:
the medical establishment
gregor mendel nikola tesla initially
they were rejected
imagine the hate they'd get if twitter
existed but
that's a long time ago surel

claims.rationale: Mendel's genetics was essentially ignored until its 1900 rediscovery — 'rejected' is too strong; 'overlooked' is more accurate. Tesla was widely celebrated during his peak (1890s-1900s) but died in near-obscurity after his later eccentricities; 'initially rejected' mischaracterizes his career arc. Partial truth with narrative compression.

claims.text: Steve Jobs and Elon Musk do not care what people think about them; they just went out and changed the world.

evidence.quote:
york freaking times
aren't new ideas worth exploring did
steve jobs care what people thought
about him
does elon musk care no they just went
out and changed the w

claims.rationale: Both Jobs and Musk are extensively documented as caring intensely about public perception — Jobs famously micromanaged product launches and media narratives, and Musk is one of the most online-reactive CEOs in history. The 'didn't care' framing is the opposite of the empirical record, even if the charitable reading is 'wasn't deterred by critics'.
